summary: Gruber criticizes Mozilla for taking until late 2014 to decide to bring Firefox to iOS, calling their years-long refusal to use Apple's WebKit rendering engine a prime example of misguided idealism. He notes that Mozilla's former CEO explicitly refused to make the policy switch in 2013, while Google had already shipped Chrome for iOS. Gruber argues that Mozilla's leadership fundamentally misunderstood why Firefox succeeded against IE — it wasn't ideological purity but practical superiority as a browser. By refusing to compromise, Mozilla bled relevance in the fastest-growing segment of the market for nearly a decade. He sees this as part of a pattern of Mozilla prioritizing ideology over practicality, citing their earlier stance against H.264 video.
key_points: Mozilla refused to ship Firefox on iOS for years because Apple required use of WebKit, calling it a principled stand
Google shipped Chrome for iOS two years earlier by accepting Apple's WebKit requirement, gaining mobile relevance Mozilla forfeited
Firefox beat IE not because of open-source ideology but because it was simply better to use — Mozilla's leadership misunderstood their own success
Mozilla's pattern of idealism over practicality has repeatedly cost them relevance, from H.264 opposition to the iOS refusal
By the time Mozilla reversed course, they had lost nearly a decade of mobile presence in the only growing part of the market
money_quotes: That it took them until 2014 to bend to practicality — iOS has been growing in popularity worldwide ever since it debuted, and Apple was never going to allow them to use their own rendering engine in an iOS app — epitomizes everything wrong with Mozilla as an organization.
I'm all for idealism, but Mozilla has been idealistic to a fault.
Their refusal to create an iOS version of Firefox unless they could use their own rendering engine was a losing hand.
They've now spent close to a decade bleeding relevance in the only part of the market that is growing: mobile.
I've long suspected that Mozilla's leadership didn't understand why Firefox beat IE.
It wasn't because Firefox was idealistically superior — open source, free of charge, superior support for open standards — but because it was just plain better to use.
key_insight: Mozilla's stubborn idealism about rendering engines on iOS cost them a decade of mobile relevance, revealing a leadership that never understood Firefox's original success was about practical superiority, not ideological purity.
